Off-Grid Datacenter LCOE

The least-cost mix of solar, wind, battery and gas backup to run an always-on, off-grid datacenter on mostly-renewable power — and the year going renewable beats burning gas, across the US and Europe.

▶  View the source code & full methodology on GitHub

Read this first. This is a stylised techno-economic model: trust the directional comparisons, not absolute numbers to better than ~±20–30%. The headline runs on synthetic (not measured) weather and a single generation site by default; both can be replaced with real reanalysis and multi-site portfolios. All numbers below are generated directly from the model's exported results.

Key findings

  • Europe — renewables already win at moderate shares. Carbon-priced, expensive EU gas (rising from $114 to $163/MWh) means a firm 70%-renewable build reaches parity ~2025, and even a 90%-renewable one by ~2029.
  • US — cheap gas is a moat at high renewable shares. Untaxed ~$46/MWh gas keeps a 90%-renewable build off parity beyond 2040 (US 90% crossover >2040); only moderate-share builds cross, in the mid-to-late 2030s.
  • A firm battery-only system tops out near ~94% renewable. Multi-day Dunkelflaute neither sun nor wind covers, and battery power can't bridge days — the last few percent always fall to gas. Going higher needs long-duration storage or hydrogen.
  • A fully gas-free, zero-carbon datacenter is feasible. A co-optimised solar+wind+battery+green-hydrogen build delivers $148→$85/MWh in the EU, crossing below gas around 2029.
  • Where you build dominates the cost. Across a poor→good site in a region the 90%-renewable delivered cost spans tens of $/MWh (the shaded bands in the figures); a multi-site portfolio softens the multi-day lulls and cuts high-renewable cost materially.
  • Off-grid is itself a premium. Staying on the grid with a renewable-energy contract sits below the off-grid high-renewable optimum in both regions — off-grid buys siting independence, at a cost.

The renewable target is the minimum share of the datacenter's yearly energy that must come from solar + wind + battery (the rest is covered by gas). Firm (always-on) workload; gas backup sized to 100% of load. "Crossover" = the first year the build's delivered cost drops below the gas baseline.

Cost trajectories

Across geographies

The same firm off-grid build, now computed at each of five large EU countries and five US states on real ERA5 reanalysis weather (2015–2025, 11 years) — one grid point per location, every real year a dispatch sample (so the curves carry real interannual variability: dark/calm years included). Within a region only the renewable resource differs — gas, carbon and technology costs are the region default — so this isolates how much where you build, and whether you build a wind park, move the cost.

Gas-backed, at a renewable target: ~55% without wind vs ~80% with wind

Solar panels are quick to permit; a wind park is a far bigger siting undertaking. A solar + battery + gas system reaches about 55% renewable comfortably without any wind; adding a wind park lifts the same firm build to ~80%. The grey dashed line is the region gas baseline; the purple dash-dot line is a small modular (nuclear) reactor — a firm, always-on reference that is never part of the optimisation. The closer the two coloured lines, the less a wind park earns you there — so in the calm, sunny Southwest (Arizona, which can't even reach 80% and is clamped to ~75%) wind barely helps, while in the windy plains and a blustery United Kingdom it pays off clearly. Note the regional contrast against firm nuclear: in the cheap-gas US the renewable builds sit well below the reactor line, whereas in carbon-priced Europe the reactor is competitive with — sometimes cheaper than — the high-renewable build.

Real hourly ERA5 at one point per location (2015–2025); solar capacity factor from horizontal irradiance ×1.25; region-default gas, carbon and technology costs; reduced optimiser fidelity (directional to ~±15% in level — the cross-site ranking and the wind gap are the robust messages). Per-state figures are in figs/locations_re/.

Fully zero-carbon, self-made hydrogen: no wind vs with wind

To drop gas entirely, replace the backstop with green hydrogen made from surplus renewables (the small residual bought from the market). These two builds are zero-carbon by construction — solar + battery + hydrogen (no wind) and solar + wind + battery + hydrogen — on the same 2015–2025 weather. Again the gap between the lines is what a wind park buys: where wind is strong (Wyoming, Iowa, Texas; the United Kingdom) it sits well below the no-wind line; where it is weak (Arizona, Virginia; Germany) the two nearly coincide and the easier-to-permit no-wind build costs little extra. The grey dashed line is the region gas baseline (it emits); the purple dash-dot line is a small modular (nuclear) reactor — the other firm zero-carbon option — which here is often competitive with the hydrogen build, especially in carbon-priced Europe.

Do you even need a wind park?

Solar panels are modular and quick to permit; a wind park is a far bigger siting and permitting undertaking. So it is worth asking what dropping wind entirely costs. This compares a solar + wind + battery build against solar + battery only (both firm, with gas backup) in 2035.

There is a hard wall. A solar + battery + gas system tops out near ~68% renewable — nights and multi-day cloud always fall to gas, and a battery cannot shift energy across days — whereas adding wind reaches ~94%. Below the wall, solar-only is only modestly pricier in the US (strong solar) and a clearer premium in Europe (weak winter sun). Bottom line: for a moderate renewable target, solar + battery alone is a reasonable, much-easier-to-build choice; pushing to high renewable fractions genuinely needs wind (or long-duration storage / hydrogen).

… and what about solar + battery + hydrogen (no wind)?

To go fully zero-carbon without a wind park, replace the gas backstop with green hydrogen. How you get the H₂ matters enormously: buying it all is expensive ($160/MWh US, $169 EU), but making it from surplus solar (a solar-heavy build + an electrolyser, buying only a few percent) brings a fully zero-carbon, wind-free datacenter to ~$105/MWh (US) / $116 (EU) by 2035 — only ~$12–17 above the with-wind zero-carbon system ($93 / $99). So wind-free zero-carbon is feasible at a modest premium — and in Europe the with-wind green-H₂ system is actually the cheapest option of all, since carbon-priced gas is dearer.

Simulated capacity factor (solar / wind)0.23 / 0.330.16 / 0.29inside Lazard CF bands

All in real 2025 USD. Costs fall over time via Wright's-Law learning curves. Full derivations, data sources and the accuracy summary are in model_documentation.md.

What is and isn't modelled

  • Default = firm, always-on: gas backup covers 100% of load during lulls, so the worst case is a known, capped fuel cost. Premium/AI workloads never shed and collapse to this case.
  • Weather is synthetic but structured (multi-day Dunkelflaute, region-specific resource and sun–wind correlation). A real-weather seam (--weather, ERA5/NSRDB) and a multi-site portfolio (--sites) are built in but opt-in.
  • Single site by default — the largest directional caveat; a geographic portfolio softens the tails and lowers high-renewable cost.
  • Not modelled: sub-hourly load variation, on-site fuel logistics, transmission.
